ARCHITECTURE_DAYLIGHT.rationale: f/11 maximizes sharpness across architectural facades. Low ISO for maximum detail and minimum noise.
ARCHITECTURE_DAYLIGHT.proTip: Use a level or electronic level to ensure perfectly straight verticals and horizons for professional results.
ARCHITECTURE_DAYLIGHT.commonMistake: Tilting the camera up to capture tall buildings creates converging verticals—step back further or use tilt-shift lens.

ARCHITECTURE_CLOUDY.rationale: Overcast sky eliminates harsh shadows on facades, revealing true materials and texture without blown highlights.
ARCHITECTURE_CLOUDY.proTip: Overcast days are ideal for showing facade materials—no shadows hide the stone, brick, or metal textures.
ARCHITECTURE_CLOUDY.commonMistake: Including a large grey sky that dominates the frame—keep sky to minimum unless cloud interest is the point.

ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_NATURAL.rationale: Tripod essential at 1/30s. f/5.6 covers the room. Expose for mid-tones and recover in post.
ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_NATURAL.proTip: Shoot HDR or blend multiple exposures to handle the extreme dynamic range between windows and interior shadows.
ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_NATURAL.commonMistake: Exposing for windows blows them out; exposing for shadows underexposes the room—bracket and blend.

ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_ARTIFICIAL.rationale: Consistent artificial light allows precise manual control. f/11 renders everything sharp including near and far elements.
ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_ARTIFICIAL.proTip: Set a custom white balance from a grey card under the dominant light source for accurate color rendering.
ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_ARTIFICIAL.commonMistake: Auto WB shifts color between frames making a consistent architectural series impossible to process.

ARCHITECTURE_TWILIGHT.rationale: Twilight gives the perfect balance between ambient sky light and building illumination—the 'sweet spot' of architectural photography.
ARCHITECTURE_TWILIGHT.proTip: Arrive 30 minutes before sunset and shoot continuously—the sweet spot window is only ~10 minutes.
ARCHITECTURE_TWILIGHT.commonMistake: Arriving at sunset—the best twilight light occurs 20-30 minutes AFTER sunset, not at sunset.

ARCHITECTURE_NIGHT_LONG_EXPOSURE.rationale: 30s captures full building illumination against a rich dark sky. f/16 creates starburst effects on lights.
ARCHITECTURE_NIGHT_LONG_EXPOSURE.proTip: Wait for light cycling to complete—some illuminated buildings have lights that pulse or change color over 10-30 seconds.
ARCHITECTURE_NIGHT_LONG_EXPOSURE.commonMistake: Using f/22 for more starburst—diffraction loss at f/22 reduces overall sharpness. f/16 is the sweet spot.

ARCHITECTURE_DETAIL_CLOSEUP.rationale: Architectural details—ornate cornices, stonework, ironwork—tell the story of a building. f/8 renders texture.
ARCHITECTURE_DETAIL_CLOSEUP.proTip: Use raking light (light at an angle to the surface) to reveal texture depth in stonework and carvings.
ARCHITECTURE_DETAIL_CLOSEUP.commonMistake: Shooting details in flat frontal light eliminates all the depth and texture that makes them beautiful.

ARCHITECTURE_WIDE_ANGLE.rationale: Wide angle lens captures the full scale and context of large architectural subjects. Use tilt-shift to correct perspective.
ARCHITECTURE_WIDE_ANGLE.proTip: Keep the camera perfectly level and crop or use perspective correction in post rather than tilting the lens.
ARCHITECTURE_WIDE_ANGLE.commonMistake: Tilting the camera upward creates keystoning (converging verticals) that makes buildings look like they're falling backward.

ARCHITECTURE_BACKLIT.rationale: Backlighting creates dramatic silhouettes of architectural forms. Iconic shapes become graphic art.
ARCHITECTURE_BACKLIT.proTip: Glass and modern buildings create spectacular backlit shots when the sun is positioned behind them.
ARCHITECTURE_BACKLIT.commonMistake: Spotmetering on the dark side of a backlit building blows out the sky and loses all detail.

ARCHITECTURE_BLACK_WHITE.rationale: B&W architecture emphasizes form, geometry, and tonal relationships. Removes distracting material colors.
ARCHITECTURE_BLACK_WHITE.proTip: Strong diagonal lines, symmetry, and dramatic shadow patterns work best for B&W architectural images.
ARCHITECTURE_BLACK_WHITE.commonMistake: Converting colorful buildings to B&W without remapping tones—the result looks flat and uninteresting.

ARCHITECTURE_REFLECTIONS_GLASS.rationale: Modern glass buildings reflect their environment. Compose to include interesting reflective content alongside structure.
ARCHITECTURE_REFLECTIONS_GLASS.proTip: Position yourself to include interesting elements (clouds, other buildings, sky) in the glass reflection.
ARCHITECTURE_REFLECTIONS_GLASS.commonMistake: Including your own reflection or an empty, uninteresting sky in the glass.

ARCHITECTURE_HIGH_CONTRAST.rationale: Intense midday light creates graphic black shadows that emphasize architectural forms and repeating patterns.
ARCHITECTURE_HIGH_CONTRAST.proTip: Look for repeating architectural patterns (balconies, arches, columns) where shadows create rhythm and geometry.
ARCHITECTURE_HIGH_CONTRAST.commonMistake: Trying to balance shadows and highlights—embrace the extremes and let shadows cut across the frame.

ARCHITECTURE_LOW_ANGLE.rationale: Looking up at buildings from a low angle creates dramatic perspective convergence. Unlike portrait photography, this is intentional.
ARCHITECTURE_LOW_ANGLE.proTip: Intentional convergence (extreme upward angle) creates a dynamic, modern look that's entirely acceptable in architectural photography.
ARCHITECTURE_LOW_ANGLE.commonMistake: Misidentifying intentional convergence (creative) with accidentally tilted camera (mistake)—be intentional.

ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_LOW_LIGHT.rationale: Historic buildings and churches have dim, atmospheric interiors. Tripod essential for sharp images.
ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_LOW_LIGHT.proTip: Use aperture priority and shoot multiple ISO levels on a tripod to find the best quality vs. noise compromise.
ARCHITECTURE_INTERIOR_LOW_LIGHT.commonMistake: Hand-holding at 1/60s in dark interiors consistently produces blurred shots—always use tripod.

ARCHITECTURE_SYMMETRICAL.rationale: Many great buildings are symmetrical—centering them perfectly creates powerful, meditative compositions.
ARCHITECTURE_SYMMETRICAL.proTip: Use the grid overlay in your viewfinder to achieve perfect symmetry. Even a 1-degree rotation ruins symmetrical shots.
ARCHITECTURE_SYMMETRICAL.commonMistake: Near-symmetry is worse than no symmetry—if you go symmetrical, make it perfect.
